text.NRSV: The words of the Teacher, the son of David, king in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: The words of the Proclaimer, son of David, king in Jerusalem.
text.KJV: 1:1 The wordes of the Preacher, the son of Dauid, King in Ierusalem.
text.NABRE: 1:1 Chapter 1 - The words of David’s son, Qoheleth, king in Jerusalem:

text.NRSV: Vanity of vanities, says the Teacher, vanity of vanities! All is vanity.
text.Rotherham: Vanity of vanities! saith the Proclaimer, vanity of vanities! all, is vanity.
text.KJV: 1:2 Uanitie of vanities, saith the Preacher, vanitie of vanities, all is vanitie.
text.NABRE: 1:2 Vanity of vanities, says Qoheleth, vanity of vanities! All things are vanity!

text.NRSV: What do people gain from all the toil at which they toil under the sun?
text.Rotherham: What profit hath Man, in all his toil wherewith he toileth under the sun?
text.KJV: 1:3 What profite hath a man of all his labour which hee taketh vnder the Sunne?
text.NABRE: 1:3 Vanity of Human Toil - What profit have we from all the toil which we toil at under the sun?

text.NRSV: A generation goes, and a generation comes, but the earth remains forever.
text.Rotherham: Generation, goeth and, generation, cometh, but, the earth, unto times age-abiding, remaineth.
text.KJV: 1:4 One generation passeth away, and another generation commeth: but the earth abideth for euer.
text.NABRE: 1:4 One generation departs and another generation comes, but the world forever stays.

text.NRSV: The sun rises and the sun goes down, and hurries to the place where it rises.
text.Rotherham: And the sun, breaketh forth, and the sun, goeth in,—yea, unto his own place, he panteth, from whence he brake forth.
text.KJV: 1:5 The Sunne also ariseth, and the Sunne goeth downe, and hasteth to the place where he arose.
text.NABRE: 1:5 The sun rises and the sun sets; then it presses on to the place where it rises.

text.NRSV: The wind blows to the south, and goes around to the north; round and round goes the wind, and on its circuits the wind returns.
text.Rotherham: Going unto the south, and circling unto the north,—circling, circling continually, is, the wind, and, over its own circuits, returneth the wind.
text.KJV: 1:6 The winde goeth toward the South, and turneth about vnto the North; it whirleth about continually, and the winde returneth againe according to his circuits.
text.NABRE: 1:6 Shifting south, then north, back and forth shifts the wind, constantly shifting its course.

text.NRSV: All streams run to the sea, but the sea is not full; to the place where the streams flow, there they continue to flow.
text.Rotherham: All the streams, flow into the sea, yet, the sea, is not full,—unto the place whither the streams flow, thither, do they again flow.
text.KJV: 1:7 All the riuers runne into the sea, yet the Sea is not full: vnto the place from whence the riuers come, thither they returne againe.
text.NABRE: 1:7 All rivers flow to the sea, yet never does the sea become full. To the place where they flow, the rivers continue to flow.

text.NRSV: All things are wearisome; more than one can express; the eye is not satisfied with seeing, or the ear filled with hearing.
text.Rotherham: All words, are weak, unable is any man to tell,—not satisfied is the eye by seeing, nor filled is the ear with hearing.
text.KJV: 1:8 All things are full of labour, man cannot vtter it: the eye is not satisfied with seeing, nor the eare filled with hearing.
text.NABRE: 1:8 All things are wearisome, too wearisome for words. The eye is not satisfied by seeing nor has the ear enough of hearing.

text.NRSV: What has been is what will be, and what has been done is what will be done; there is nothing new under the sun.
text.Rotherham: That which hath been, is the same that shall be, and, that which hath been done, is the same that shall be done,—and there is, nothing new, under the sun.
text.KJV: 1:9 The thing that hath beene, it is that which shall be: and that which is done, is that which shall be done; and there is no new thing vnder the sunne.
text.NABRE: 1:9 What has been, that will be; what has been done, that will be done. Nothing is new under the sun!

text.NRSV: Is there a thing of which it is said, “See, this is new”? It has been already, in the ages before us.
text.Rotherham: Is there a thing, of which it can be said, See here, it is, new? Already, hath it been, for ages, [it is something] which was before us.
text.KJV: 1:10 Is there any thing, whereof it may be sayd, See, this is new? it hath beene already of olde time, which was before vs.
text.NABRE: 1:10 Even the thing of which we say, “See, this is new!” has already existed in the ages that preceded us.

text.NRSV: The people of long ago are not remembered, nor will there be any remembrance of people yet to come by those who come after them.
text.Rotherham: There is, no remembrance, of the thing before,—nor, even of the things after, which shall be, will there be any remembrance, with them who shall come after.
text.KJV: 1:11 There is no remembrance of former things; neither shall there bee any remembrance of things that are to come, with those that shall come after.
text.NABRE: 1:11 There is no remembrance of past generations; nor will future generations be remembered by those who come after them.

text.NRSV: I, the Teacher, when king over Israel in Jerusalem,
text.Rotherham: I, the Proclaimer, was king over Israel, in Jerusalem.
text.KJV: 1:12 ¶ I the Preacher was king ouer Israel in Ierusalem.
text.NABRE: 1:12 I. Qoheleth’s Investigation of Life - Twofold Introduction. I, Qoheleth, was king over Israel in Jerusalem,

text.NRSV: applied my mind to seek and to search out by wisdom all that is done under heaven; it is an unhappy business that God has given to human beings to be busy with.
text.Rotherham: And I gave my heart to seek and to search out, wisely, concerning all things which are done under the heavens,—the same, is the vexatious employment God hath given to the sons of men, to work toilsomely therein,
text.KJV: 1:13 And I gaue my heart to seeke and search out by wisedome, concerning all things that are done vnder heauen: this sore trauell hath God giuen to the sonnes of man, to be exercised therewith.
text.NABRE: 1:13 and I applied my mind to search and investigate in wisdom all things that are done under the sun. A bad business God has given to human beings to be busied with.

text.NRSV: I saw all the deeds that are done under the sun; and see, all is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: I saw all the works which were done under the sun,—and lo! all, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 1:14 I haue seene all the workes that are done vnder the Sunne, and behold, all is vanitie, and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 1:14 I have seen all things that are done under the sun, and behold, all is vanity and a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: What is crooked cannot be made straight, and what is lacking cannot be numbered.
text.Rotherham: That which is crooked, cannot be straight,—and, that which is wanting, cannot be reckoned.
text.KJV: 1:15 That which is crooked, cannot be made straight: and that which is wanting cannot be numbred.
text.NABRE: 1:15 What is crooked cannot be made straight, and you cannot count what is not there.

text.NRSV: I said to myself, “I have acquired great wisdom, surpassing all who were over Jerusalem before me; and my mind has had great experience of wisdom and knowledge.”
text.Rotherham: Spake, I, in my heart, saying, As for me, lo! I have become great, and have gathered wisdom, beyond any one who hath been before me over Jerusalem,—and, my heart, hath seen much wisdom and knowledge:
text.KJV: 1:16 I communed with mine owne heart, saying, Loe, I am come to great estate, and haue gotten more wisedome then all they that haue beene before me in Ierusalem: yea my heart had great experience of wisedome & knowledge.
text.NABRE: 1:16 Though I said to myself, “See, I have greatly increased my wisdom beyond all who were before me in Jerusalem, and my mind has broad experience of wisdom and knowledge,”

text.NRSV: And I applied my mind to know wisdom and to know madness and folly. I perceived that this also is but a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: yea I have given my heart, to know wisdom, and to know madness, and folly,—I know that, even this, is a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 1:17 And I gaue my heart to know wisedome, and to know madnesse and folly: I perceiued that this also is vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 1:17 yet when I applied my mind to know wisdom and knowledge, madness and folly, I learned that this also is a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: For in much wisdom is much vexation, and those who increase knowledge increase sorrow.
text.Rotherham: For, in much wisdom, is much vexation,—and, he that increaseth knowledge, increaseth pain.
text.KJV: 1:18 For in much wisedome is much griefe: and hee that increaseth knowledge, increaseth sorrow.
text.NABRE: 1:18 For in much wisdom there is much sorrow; whoever increases knowledge increases grief.

text.NRSV: I said to myself, “Come now, I will make a test of pleasure; enjoy yourself.” But again, this also was vanity.
text.Rotherham: Said, I, in my heart, Come now! I will prove thee with gladness, and look thou on blessedness,—but lo! even that, was vanity.
text.KJV: 2:1 I said in mine heart, Goe to now, I wil prooue thee with mirth, therfore enioy pleasure: and behold, this also is vanitie.
text.NABRE: 2:1 Chapter 2 - Study of Pleasure-seeking. I said in my heart, “Come, now, let me try you with pleasure and the enjoyment of good things.” See, this too was vanity.

text.NRSV: I said of laughter, “It is mad,” and of pleasure, “What use is it?”
text.Rotherham: Of laughter, I said, Madness! and, of mirth, What can it do?
text.KJV: 2:2 I saide of laughter, It is mad: and of mirth, What doeth it?
text.NABRE: 2:2 Of laughter I said: “Mad!” and of mirth: “What good does this do?”

text.NRSV: I searched with my mind how to cheer my body with wine—my mind still guiding me with wisdom—and how to lay hold on folly, until I might see what was good for mortals to do under heaven during the few days of their life.
text.Rotherham: I sought out with my heart, to cherish with wine, my flesh,—but, my heart, was to guide with wisdom, even in laying hold of folly, until I should see which was blessedness for the sons of men, as to that which they could do, under the heavens, during the number of the days of their life.
text.KJV: 2:3 I sought in mine heart to giue my selfe vnto wine, (yet acquainting mine heart with wisedome) and to lay hold on folly, till I might see what was that good for the sonnes of men, which they should doe vnder the heauen all the dayes of their life.
text.NABRE: 2:3 Guided by wisdom, I probed with my mind how to beguile my senses with wine and take up folly, until I should understand what is good for human beings to do under the heavens during the limited days of their lives.

text.NRSV: I made great works; I built houses and planted vineyards for myself;
text.Rotherham: I enlarged my works,—I built me houses, I planted me vineyards;
text.KJV: 2:4 I made me great workes, I builded mee houses, I planted mee Uineyards.
text.NABRE: 2:4 I undertook great works; I built myself houses and planted vineyards;

text.NRSV: I made myself gardens and parks, and planted in them all kinds of fruit trees.
text.Rotherham: I made me gardens, and parks,—I planted in them trees of every kind of fruit;
text.KJV: 2:5 I made mee gardens & orchards, and I planted trees in them of all kinde of fruits.
text.NABRE: 2:5 I made gardens and parks, and in them set out fruit trees of all sorts.

text.NRSV: I made myself pools from which to water the forest of growing trees.
text.Rotherham: I made me pools of water,—to irrigate therefrom the thick-set saplings growing up into trees:
text.KJV: 2:6 I made mee pooles of water, to water therewith the wood that bringeth foorth trees:
text.NABRE: 2:6 And I constructed for myself reservoirs to water a flourishing woodland.

text.NRSV: I bought male and female slaves, and had slaves who were born in my house; I also had great possessions of herds and flocks, more than any who had been before me in Jerusalem.
text.Rotherham: I acquired, men-servants and women-servants, and, the children of the household, were mine,—also possessions, herds and flocks in abundance, were mine, beyond all who had been before me in Jerusalem;
text.KJV: 2:7 I got me seruants and maydens, and had seruants borne in my house; also I had great possessions of great and small cattell, aboue all that were in Ierusalem before me.
text.NABRE: 2:7 I acquired male and female slaves, and had slaves who were born in my house. I also owned vast herds of cattle and flocks of sheep, more than all who had been before me in Jerusalem.

text.NRSV: I also gathered for myself silver and gold and the treasure of kings and of the provinces; I got singers, both men and women, and delights of the flesh, and many concubines.
text.Rotherham: I heaped me up, both silver and gold, and the peculiar treasure of kings, and provinces,—I provided me singing-men and singing-women, and the delights of the sons of men, a wife and wives.
text.KJV: 2:8 I gathered mee also siluer and gold, and the peculiar treasure of kings and of the prouinces: I gate mee men singers and women singers, and the delights of the sonnes of men, as musical instruments, and that of all sorts.
text.NABRE: 2:8 I amassed for myself silver and gold, and the treasures of kings and provinces. I provided for myself male and female singers and delights of men, many women.

text.NRSV: So I became great and surpassed all who were before me in Jerusalem; also my wisdom remained with me.
text.Rotherham: So I became great, and increased, more than any one who had been before me in Jerusalem,—moreover, my wisdom, remained with me;
text.KJV: 2:9 So I was great, and increased more then all that were before mee in Ierusalem; also my wisedome remained with me.
text.NABRE: 2:9 I accumulated much more than all others before me in Jerusalem; my wisdom, too, stayed with me.

text.NRSV: Whatever my eyes desired I did not keep from them; I kept my heart from no pleasure, for my heart found pleasure in all my toil, and this was my reward for all my toil.
text.Rotherham: and, nothing that mine eyes asked, withheld I from them,—I did not keep back my heart from any gladness, for, my heart, obtained gladness out of all my toil, and so, this, was my portion, out of all my toil.
text.KJV: 2:10 And whatsoeuer mine eyes desired, I kept not from them; I withheld not my heart from any ioy: for my heart reioyced in all my labour; and this was my portion of all my labour.
text.NABRE: 2:10 Nothing that my eyes desired did I deny them, nor did I deprive myself of any joy; rather, my heart rejoiced in the fruit of all my toil. This was my share for all my toil.

text.NRSV: Then I considered all that my hands had done and the toil I had spent in doing it, and again, all was vanity and a chasing after wind, and there was nothing to be gained under the sun.
text.Rotherham: When, I, looked upon all my works, which my hands had made, and on my toil, whereon I had toilsomely wrought, then lo! all, was vanity, and feeding on wind, and there was no profit under the sun.
text.KJV: 2:11 Then I looked on all the workes that my hands had wrought, and on the labour that I had laboured to doe: and behold, all was vanitie, and vexation of spirit, and there was no profit vnder the Sunne.
text.NABRE: 2:11 But when I turned to all the works that my hands had wrought, and to the fruit of the toil for which I had toiled so much, see! all was vanity and a chase after wind. There is no profit under the sun.

text.NRSV: So I turned to consider wisdom and madness and folly; for what can the one do who comes after the king? Only what has already been done.
text.Rotherham: Thus turned, I, to look at wisdom, and madness and folly,—for what can the man [do more] who cometh after the king? [save] that which, already, men have done.
text.KJV: 2:12 ¶ And I turned my selfe to behold wisedome, and madnesse and folly: for what can the man doe, that commeth after the king? euen that which hath bene already done.
text.NABRE: 2:12 What about one who succeeds a king? He can do only what has already been done. Study of Wisdom and Folly. I went on to the consideration of wisdom, madness and folly.

text.NRSV: Then I saw that wisdom excels folly as light excels darkness.
text.Rotherham: Then saw, I, that wisdom doth excel folly,—as far as light excelleth darkness.
text.KJV: 2:13 Then I saw that wisedome excelleth folly, as farre as light excelleth darkenesse.
text.NABRE: 2:13 And I saw that wisdom has as much profit over folly as light has over darkness.

text.NRSV: The wise have eyes in their head, but fools walk in darkness. Yet I perceived that the same fate befalls all of them.
text.Rotherham: As for the wise man, his eyes, are in his head, whereas, the dullard, in darkness, doth walk,—but, I myself, knew that, one destiny, happeneth to them, all.
text.KJV: 2:14 The wise mans eyes are in his head, but the foole walketh in darknes: and I my selfe perceiued also that one euent happeneth to them all.
text.NABRE: 2:14 Wise people have eyes in their heads, but fools walk in darkness. Yet I knew that the same lot befalls both.

text.NRSV: Then I said to myself, “What happens to the fool will happen to me also; why then have I been so very wise?” And I said to myself that this also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: Then said, I, in my heart, As it happeneth to the dullard, even to me, will it happen, but wherefore, then, became, I, wise to excess? Therefore spake I, in my heart, Even this, is vanity.
text.KJV: 2:15 Then said I in my heart, As it happeneth to the foole, so it happeneth euen to me, and why was I then more wise? then I said in my heart, That this also is vanitie.
text.NABRE: 2:15 So I said in my heart, if the fool’s lot is to befall me also, why should I be wise? Where is the profit? And in my heart I decided that this too is vanity.

text.NRSV: For there is no enduring remembrance of the wise or of fools, seeing that in the days to come all will have been long forgotten. How can the wise die just like fools?
text.Rotherham: For there is no remembrance of a wise man, more than of a dullard, unto times age-abiding,—seeing that, already, in the days to come, all hath been forgotten, how then cometh it that the wise man dieth equally with the dullard?
text.KJV: 2:16 For there is no remembrance of the wise, more then of the foole for euer; seeing that which now is, in the dayes to come shall be forgotten; and how dieth the wise man? as the foole.
text.NABRE: 2:16 The wise person will have no more abiding remembrance than the fool; for in days to come both will have been forgotten. How is it that the wise person dies like the fool!

text.NRSV: So I hated life, because what is done under the sun was grievous to me; for all is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Therefore I hated life, for, a vexation unto me, was the work which was done under the sun,—for, all, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 2:17 Therefore I hated life, because the worke that is wrought vnder the Sunne is grieuous vnto mee: for all is vanitie, and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 2:17 Therefore I detested life, since for me the work that is done under the sun is bad; for all is vanity and a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: I hated all my toil in which I had toiled under the sun, seeing that I must leave it to those who come after me
text.Rotherham: Therefore hated, I, all my toil, wherein I was toiling, under the sun,—in that I should leave it for the man who should come after me;
text.KJV: 2:18 ¶ Yea I hated all my labour which I had taken vnder the Sunne: because I should leaue it vnto the man that shalbe after mee.
text.NABRE: 2:18 Study of the Fruits of Toil - To Others the Profits. And I detested all the fruits of my toil under the sun, because I must leave them to the one who is to come after me.

text.NRSV: —and who knows whether they will be wise or foolish? Yet they will be master of all for which I toiled and used my wisdom under the sun. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: and who could know whether a, wise man, he would be or a foolish, and yet he would lord it over all my toil, wherein I had toiled and wherein I had acted wisely, under the sun,—even this, was vanity.
text.KJV: 2:19 And who knoweth whether he shall be a wise man or a foole? yet shall he haue rule ouer all my labour, wherein I haue laboured, and wherein I haue shewed my selfe wise vnder the Sunne. This is also vanitie.
text.NABRE: 2:19 And who knows whether that one will be wise or a fool? Yet that one will take control of all the fruits of my toil and wisdom under the sun. This also is vanity.

text.NRSV: So I turned and gave my heart up to despair concerning all the toil of my labors under the sun,
text.Rotherham: Then resolved I, to give my heart over to despair,—concerning all the toil, wherein I had toiled, under the sun.
text.KJV: 2:20 Therefore I went about to cause my heart to despaire of all the labour which I tooke vnder the Sunne.
text.NABRE: 2:20 So my heart turned to despair over all the fruits of my toil under the sun.

text.NRSV: because sometimes one who has toiled with wisdom and knowledge and skill must leave all to be enjoyed by another who did not toil for it. This also is vanity and a great evil.
text.Rotherham: For here is a man, whose toil hath been with wisdom and with knowledge and with skill,—yet, to a man who hath not toiled therein, shall he leave it as his portion, even this, was vanity and a great vexation.
text.KJV: 2:21 For there is a man whose labour is in wisedome and in knowledge, and in equitie: yet to a man that hath not laboured therein, shall hee leaue it for his portion; This also is vanitie, and a great euill.
text.NABRE: 2:21 For here is one who has toiled with wisdom and knowledge and skill, and that one’s legacy must be left to another who has not toiled for it. This also is vanity and a great evil.

text.NRSV: What do mortals get from all the toil and strain they endure under the sun?
text.Rotherham: For what hath the man for all his toil, and for the striving of his heart,—wherein, he himself, toiled under the sun?
text.KJV: 2:22 For what hath man of all his labour, and of the vexation of his heart wherein hee hath laboured vnder the Sunne?
text.NABRE: 2:22 For what profit comes to mortals from all the toil and anxiety of heart with which they toil under the sun?

text.NRSV: For all their days are full of pain, and their work is a vexation; even at night their minds do not rest. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: For, all his days, are pains, and, vexatious, is his employment, even in the night, his heart lieth not down,—even this, was, vanity.
text.KJV: 2:23 For all his dayes are sorrowes, and his traueile, griefe; yea his heart taketh not rest in the night. This is also vanitie.
text.NABRE: 2:23 Every day sorrow and grief are their occupation; even at night their hearts are not at rest. This also is vanity.

text.NRSV: There is nothing better for mortals than to eat and drink, and find enjoyment in their toil. This also, I saw, is from the hand of God;
text.Rotherham: There was nothing more blessed for Man [than] that he should eat and drink, and see his desireth for blessedness in his toil,—even this, saw, I myself, that, from the hand of God, it was.
text.KJV: 2:24 ¶ There is nothing better for a man, then that he should eat and drinke, and that he should make his soule enioy good in his labour. This also I saw, that it was from the hand of God.
text.NABRE: 2:24 There is nothing better for mortals than to eat and drink and provide themselves with good things from their toil. Even this, I saw, is from the hand of God.

text.NRSV: for apart from him who can eat or who can have enjoyment?
text.Rotherham: For who could eat and who could enjoy, so well as I?
text.KJV: 2:25 For who can eate? or who else can hasten hereunto more then I?
text.NABRE: 2:25 For who can eat or drink apart from God?

text.NRSV: For to the one who pleases him God gives wisdom and knowledge and joy; but to the sinner he gives the work of gathering and heaping, only to give to one who pleases God. This also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: For, to a man who is good before him, hath he given wisdom and knowledge and gladness,—whereas, to the sinner, he hath given employment, to gather and heap up, to give to one who is good before God, even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 2:26 For God giueth to a man that is good in his sight, wisedome, and knowledge, and ioy: but to the sinner hee giueth traueile, to gather and to heape vp that he may giue to him that is good before God: This also is vanitie and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 2:26 For to the one who pleases God, he gives wisdom and knowledge and joy; but to the one who displeases, God gives the task of gathering possessions for the one who pleases God. This also is vanity and a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: For everything there is a season, and a time for every matter under heaven:
text.Rotherham: For, every thing, there is a season,—and a time for every pursuit, under the heavens:—
text.KJV: 3:1 To euery thing there is a season, and a time to euery purpose vnder the heauen.
text.NABRE: 3:1 Chapter 3 No One Can Determine the Right Time to Act - There is an appointed time for everything, and a time for every affair under the heavens.

text.NRSV: a time to be born, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to pluck up what is planted;
text.Rotherham: A time to be born, and a time to die,—A time to plant, and a time to uproot what is planted;
text.KJV: 3:2 A time to be borne, and a time to die: a time to plant, and a time to pluck vp that which is planted.
text.NABRE: 3:2 A time to give birth, and a time to die; a time to plant, and a time to uproot the plant.

text.NRSV: a time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to break down, and a time to build up;
text.Rotherham: A time to kill, and a time to heal,—A time to break down, and a time to build up;
text.KJV: 3:3 A time to kill, and a time to heale: a time to breake downe, and a time to build vp.
text.NABRE: 3:3 A time to kill, and a time to heal; a time to tear down, and a time to build.

text.NRSV: a time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance;
text.Rotherham: A time to weep, and a time to laugh,—A time to wail, and a time to dance for joy;
text.KJV: 3:4 A time to weepe, and a time to laugh: a time to mourne, and a time to dance.
text.NABRE: 3:4 A time to weep, and a time to laugh; a time to mourn, and a time to dance.

text.NRSV: a time to throw away stones, and a time to gather stones together; a time to embrace, and a time to refrain from embracing;
text.Rotherham: A time to cast away stones, and a time to heap up stones,—A time to embrace, and a time to be far from loving embrace;
text.KJV: 3:5 A time to cast away stones, and a time to gather stones together: a time to imbrace, and a time to refraine from imbracing.
text.NABRE: 3:5 A time to scatter stones, and a time to gather them; a time to embrace, and a time to be far from embraces.

text.NRSV: a time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to throw away;
text.Rotherham: A time to seek, and a time to give up as lost,—A time to keep, and a time to cast away;
text.KJV: 3:6 A time to get, and a time to lose: a time to keepe, and a time to cast away.
text.NABRE: 3:6 A time to seek, and a time to lose; a time to keep, and a time to cast away.

text.NRSV: a time to tear, and a time to sew; a time to keep silence, and a time to speak;
text.Rotherham: A time to rend, and a time to sew,—A time to be silent, and a time to speak;
text.KJV: 3:7 A time to rent, and a time to sow: a time to keepe silence, and a time to speake.
text.NABRE: 3:7 A time to rend, and a time to sew; a time to be silent, and a time to speak.

text.NRSV: a time for love, and a time for hate; a time for war, and a time for peace.
text.Rotherham: A time to love and a time to hate,—A time of war, and a time of peace.
text.KJV: 3:8 A time to loue, and a time to hate: a time of warre, and a time of peace.
text.NABRE: 3:8 A time to love, and a time to hate; a time of war, and a time of peace.

text.NRSV: What gain have the workers from their toil?
text.Rotherham: What profit hath he that worketh, in that wherein, himself, hath toiled?
text.KJV: 3:9 What profite hath hee that worketh, in that wherein he laboureth?
text.NABRE: 3:9 What profit have workers from their toil?

text.NRSV: I have seen the business that God has given to everyone to be busy with.
text.Rotherham: I looked at the employment which God hath given to the sons of men, to work therein:
text.KJV: 3:10 I haue seene the trauaile which God hath giuen to the sonnes of men, to be exercised in it.
text.NABRE: 3:10 I have seen the business that God has given to mortals to be busied about.

text.NRSV: He has made everything suitable for its time; moreover he has put a sense of past and future into their minds, yet they cannot find out what God has done from the beginning to the end.
text.Rotherham: Everything, hath he made beautiful in its own time,—also, intelligence, hath he put in their heart, without which men could not find out the work which God hath wrought, from the beginning even unto the end.
text.KJV: 3:11 He hath made euery thing beautifull in his time: also hee hath set the world in their heart, so that no man can finde out the worke that God maketh from the beginning to the end.
text.NABRE: 3:11 God has made everything appropriate to its time, but has put the timeless into their hearts so they cannot find out, from beginning to end, the work which God has done.

text.NRSV: I know that there is nothing better for them than to be happy and enjoy themselves as long as they live;
text.Rotherham: I know that there is no blessedness in them,—save to be glad, and to do well with one’s life.
text.KJV: 3:12 I know that there is no good in them, but for a man to reioyce, and to doe good in his life.
text.NABRE: 3:12 I recognized that there is nothing better than to rejoice and to do well during life.

text.NRSV: moreover, it is God’s gift that all should eat and drink and take pleasure in all their toil.
text.Rotherham: Though indeed, that any man should eat and drink, and see blessedness, in all his toil, it is, the gift of God.
text.KJV: 3:13 And also that euery man should eate and drinke, and enioy the good of all his labour: it is the gift of God.
text.NABRE: 3:13 Moreover, that all can eat and drink and enjoy the good of all their toil—this is a gift of God.

text.NRSV: I know that whatever God does endures forever; nothing can be added to it, nor anything taken from it; God has done this, so that all should stand in awe before him.
text.Rotherham: I know, that, whatsoever God doeth, the same, shall be age-abiding, unto it, there is nothing to add, and, from it, there is nothing to take away,—and, God, hath done it, that men should stand in awe before him.
text.KJV: 3:14 I know that whatsoeuer God doeth, it shalbe for euer: nothing can be put to it, nor any thing taken from it: and God doth it, that men should feare before him.
text.NABRE: 3:14 I recognized that whatever God does will endure forever; there is no adding to it, or taking from it. Thus has God done that he may be revered.

text.NRSV: That which is, already has been; that which is to be, already is; and God seeks out what has gone by.
text.Rotherham: That which was, already, had been, and, that which shall be, already, shall have been,—but, God, seeketh that which hath been chased away.
text.KJV: 3:15 That which hath beene, is now: and that which is to be, hath alreadie beene, and God requireth that which is past.
text.NABRE: 3:15 What now is has already been; what is to be, already is: God retrieves what has gone by.

text.NRSV: Moreover I saw under the sun that in the place of justice, wickedness was there, and in the place of righteousness, wickedness was there as well.
text.Rotherham: Then, again, I saw under the sun, the place of justice, that there was lawlessness, and, the place of righteousness, that there was lawlessness.
text.KJV: 3:16 ¶ And moreouer, I sawe vnder the Sunne the place of iudgement, that wickednesse was there; and the place of righteousnesse, that iniquitie was there.
text.NABRE: 3:16 The Problem of Retribution. And still under the sun in the judgment place I saw wickedness, and wickedness also in the seat of justice.

text.NRSV: I said in my heart, God will judge the righteous and the wicked, for he has appointed a time for every matter, and for every work.
text.Rotherham: Said, I, in my heart, Both the righteous and the lawless, will God judge,—for [there will be] a time for every pursuit, and concerning every work—there.
text.KJV: 3:17 I said in mine heart, God shall iudge the righteous and the wicked: for there is a time there, for euery purpose and for euery worke.
text.NABRE: 3:17 I said in my heart, both the just and the wicked God will judge, since a time is set for every affair and for every work.

text.NRSV: I said in my heart with regard to human beings that God is testing them to show that they are but animals.
text.Rotherham: Said, I, in my heart, as concerning the sons of men, That God was minded to prove them,—and that they might see, that they were beasts, of themselves.
text.KJV: 3:18 I said in my heart concerning the estate of the sonnes of men, that God might manifest them, and that they might see that they themselues are beasts.
text.NABRE: 3:18 I said in my heart: As for human beings, it is God’s way of testing them and of showing that they are in themselves like beasts.

text.NRSV: For the fate of humans and the fate of animals is the same; as one dies, so dies the other. They all have the same breath, and humans have no advantage over the animals; for all is vanity.
text.Rotherham: For, as regardeth the destiny of the sons of men and the destiny of beasts, one fate, have they, as dieth the one, so, dieth the other, and, one spirit, have they all,—and, the pre-eminence of man over beast, is nothing, for, all, were vanity:
text.KJV: 3:19 For that which befalleth the sonnes of men, befalleth beastes, euen one thing befalleth them: as the one dieth, so dieth the other; yea they haue all one breath, so that a man hath no preheminence aboue a beast; for all is vanitie.
text.NABRE: 3:19 For the lot of mortals and the lot of beasts is the same lot: The one dies as well as the other. Both have the same life breath. Human beings have no advantage over beasts, but all is vanity.

text.NRSV: All go to one place; all are from the dust, and all turn to dust again.
text.Rotherham: all, go unto one place,—all, came from the dust, and all, return to the dust.
text.KJV: 3:20 All goe vnto one place, all are of the dust, and all turne to dust againe.
text.NABRE: 3:20 Both go to the same place; both were made from the dust, and to the dust they both return.

text.NRSV: Who knows whether the human spirit goes upward and the spirit of animals goes downward to the earth?
text.Rotherham: Who knoweth the spirit of the sons of men, whether it, ascendeth, above,—or the spirit of the beast, whether it, descendeth, below, to the earth?
text.KJV: 3:21 Who knoweth the spirit of man that goeth vpward; and the spirit of the beast that goeth downeward to the earth?
text.NABRE: 3:21 Who knows if the life breath of mortals goes upward and the life breath of beasts goes earthward?

text.NRSV: So I saw that there is nothing better than that all should enjoy their work, for that is their lot; who can bring them to see what will be after them?
text.Rotherham: So I saw, that there was nothing better than that a man should be glad in his works, for, that, is his portion,—for who can bring him in, to look upon that which shall be after him?
text.KJV: 3:22 Wherefore I perceiue that there is nothing better, then that a man should reioyce in his owne workes: for that is his portion; for who shall bring him to see what shalbe after him?
text.NABRE: 3:22 And I saw that there is nothing better for mortals than to rejoice in their work; for this is their lot. Who will let them see what is to come after them?

text.NRSV: Again I saw all the oppressions that are practiced under the sun. Look, the tears of the oppressed—with no one to comfort them! On the side of their oppressors there was power—with no one to comfort them.
text.Rotherham: Then again, I, considered all the oppressive deeds which were done under the sun,—and lo! the tears of the oppressed, and they have no comforter, and, on the side of their oppressing, is power, and they have no comforter.
text.KJV: 4:1 So I returned, and considered all the oppressions that are done vnder the sunne; & behold the teares of such as were oppressed, and they had no comforter: and on the side of their oppressours there was power, but they had no comforter.
text.NABRE: 4:1 Chapter 4 - Vanity of Toil. Again I saw all the oppressions that take place under the sun: the tears of the victims with none to comfort them! From the hand of their oppressors comes violence, and there is none to comfort them!

text.NRSV: And I thought the dead, who have already died, more fortunate than the living, who are still alive;
text.Rotherham: So, I, pronounced happy the dead, who were, already, dead,—more than the living, who were living, still;
text.KJV: 4:2 Wherefore I praised the dead which are already dead, more then the liuing which are yet aliue.
text.NABRE: 4:2 And those now dead, I declared more fortunate in death than are the living to be still alive.

text.NRSV: but better than both is the one who has not yet been, and has not seen the evil deeds that are done under the sun.
text.Rotherham: and, as better than both, him who had not yet come into being,—who had not seen the vexatious work, which was done under the sun.
text.KJV: 4:3 Yea better is he then both they, which hath not yet been, who hath not seene the euill worke that is done vnder the Sunne.
text.NABRE: 4:3 And better off than both is the yet unborn, who has not seen the wicked work that is done under the sun.

text.NRSV: Then I saw that all toil and all skill in work come from one person’s envy of another. This also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Then saw, I, all the toil and all the skill of the work, that, for this, a man was envied of his neighbour,—even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 4:4 ¶ Againe I considered all trauaile, and euery right worke, that for this a man is enuied of his neighbour: this is also vanitie, and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 4:4 Then I saw that all toil and skillful work is the rivalry of one person with another. This also is vanity and a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: Fools fold their hands and consume their own flesh.
text.Rotherham: The dullard, claspeth his hands, and consumeth his own flesh.
text.KJV: 4:5 The foole foldeth his hands together, and eateth his owne flesh.
text.NABRE: 4:5 “Fools fold their arms and consume their own flesh”—

text.NRSV: Better is a handful with quiet than two handfuls with toil, and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Better a handful—with quietness,—than both hands, full—with toil, and feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 4:6 Better is an handfull with quietnesse, then both the hands full with trauell and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 4:6 Better is one handful with tranquility than two with toil and a chase after wind!

text.NRSV: Again, I saw vanity under the sun:
text.Rotherham: Then again, I, looked at a vain thing under the sun:—
text.KJV: 4:7 ¶ Then I returned, and I saw vanitie vnder the Sunne.
text.NABRE: 4:7 Companions and Successors. Again I saw this vanity under the sun:

text.NRSV: the case of solitary individuals, without companion or child or brother, yet there is no end to all their toil, and their eyes are never satisfied with riches. “For whom am I toiling,” they ask, “and depriving myself of pleasure?” This also is vanity and an unhappy business.
text.Rotherham: Here is one, without a second, even son or brother, he hath none, yet is there no end to all his toil, even his eye, is not satisfied with riches,—neither [saith he] For whom, am I toiling, and letting my soul want good? Even this, was vanity, yea a vexatious employment, it was!
text.KJV: 4:8 There is one alone, and there is not a second; yea, he hath neither childe nor brother: yet is there no end of all his labour, neither is his eye satisfied with riches, neither sayth hee, For whom doe I labour, and bereaue my soule of good? this is also vanitie, yea it is a sore trauell.
text.NABRE: 4:8 those all alone with no companion, with neither child nor sibling—with no end to all their toil, and no satisfaction from riches. For whom do I toil and deprive myself of good things? This also is vanity and a bad business.

text.NRSV: Two are better than one, because they have a good reward for their toil.
text.Rotherham: Better are two, than one,—in that they have a good reward for their toil.
text.KJV: 4:9 ¶ Two are better then one; because they haue a good reward for their labour.
text.NABRE: 4:9 Two are better than one: They get a good wage for their toil.

text.NRSV: For if they fall, one will lift up the other; but woe to one who is alone and falls and does not have another to help.
text.Rotherham: For, if the one should fall, [the other] would raise up his companion,—but alas! for him who is alone when he falleth, with no second to raise him up!
text.KJV: 4:10 For if they fall, the one will lift vp his fellow; but woe to him that is alone, when he falleth: for he hath not another to helpe him vp.
text.NABRE: 4:10 If the one falls, the other will help the fallen one. But woe to the solitary person! If that one should fall, there is no other to help.

text.NRSV: Again, if two lie together, they keep warm; but how can one keep warm alone?
text.Rotherham: Moreover, if two lie together, then have they warmth,—but how can, one, have warmth?
text.KJV: 4:11 Againe, if two lye together, then they haue heate; but howe can one be warme alone?
text.NABRE: 4:11 So also, if two sleep together, they keep each other warm. How can one alone keep warm?

text.NRSV: And though one might prevail against another, two will withstand one. A threefold cord is not quickly broken.
text.Rotherham: And, though an enemy should prevail against one, two, might make a stand before him,—and, a threefold cord, cannot soon be broken.
text.KJV: 4:12 And if one preuaile against him, two shall withstand him; and a threefold coard is not quickly broken.
text.NABRE: 4:12 Where one alone may be overcome, two together can resist. A three-ply cord is not easily broken.

text.NRSV: Better is a poor but wise youth than an old but foolish king, who will no longer take advice.
text.Rotherham: Better a boy poor and wise,—than a king, old and stupid, who knoweth not how to take warning any longer.
text.KJV: 4:13 ¶ Better is a poore and a wise child, then an old and foolish king who will no more be admonished.
text.NABRE: 4:13 Better is a poor but wise youth than an old but foolish king who no longer knows caution;

text.NRSV: One can indeed come out of prison to reign, even though born poor in the kingdom.
text.Rotherham: For, out of prison, came he forth to reign, yea, even in his own kingdom, was he born poor.
text.KJV: 4:14 For out of prison hee commeth to raigne, whereas also he that is borne in his kingdome, becommeth poore.
text.NABRE: 4:14 for from a prison house he came forth to reign; despite his kingship he was born poor.

text.NRSV: I saw all the living who, moving about under the sun, follow that youth, the king’s successor;
text.Rotherham: I saw all the living, who were going hither and thither under the sun,—[that they were] with the boy who was to be the second, who was to stand in the other’s place:—
text.KJV: 4:15 I considered all the liuing which walke vnder the sunne, with the second child that shall stand vp in his stead.
text.NABRE: 4:15 I saw all the living, those who move about under the sun, with the second youth who will succeed him.

text.NRSV: there was no end to all those people whom he led. Yet those who come later will not rejoice in him. Surely this also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: There was no end to all the people, to all before whom he came, yet, they who should come later, would not rejoice in him,—surely, even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 4:16 There is no end of all the people, euen of all that haue beene before them: they also that come after, shall not reioyce in him: surely this also is vanitie, and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 4:16 There is no end to all this people, to all who were before them; yet the later generations will not have joy in him. This also is vanity and a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: Guard your steps when you go to the house of God; to draw near to listen is better than the sacrifice offered by fools; for they do not know how to keep from doing evil.
text.Rotherham: Keep thy foot, when thou goest unto the house of God, and be more ready to hear, than dullards to offer sacrifice,—for they make no acknowledgment of doing wrong.
text.KJV: 5:1 Kepe thy foote when thou goest to the house of God, and be more ready to heare, then to giue the sacrifice of fooles: for they consider not that they doe euill.
text.NABRE: 5:1 Chapter 5 - Be not hasty in your utterance and let not your heart be quick to utter a promise in God’s presence. God is in heaven and you are on earth; therefore let your words be few.

text.NRSV: Never be rash with your mouth, nor let your heart be quick to utter a word before God, for God is in heaven, and you upon earth; therefore let your words be few.
text.Rotherham: Be not rash with thy mouth, and, with thy heart, be not in haste to bring forth a word, before God,—for, God, is in the heavens, and, thou, upon the earth, for this cause, let thy words be few.
text.KJV: 5:2 Be not rash with thy mouth, and let not thine heart be hasty to vtter any thing before God: for God is in heauen, and thou vpon earth: therefore let thy words be few.
text.NABRE: 5:2 As dreams come along with many cares, so a fool’s voice along with a multitude of words.

text.NRSV: For dreams come with many cares, and a fool’s voice with many words.
text.Rotherham: For a dream cometh through the multitude of business,—and, the voice of a dullard, is with a multitude of words.
text.KJV: 5:3 For a dreame commeth through the multitude of businesse, and a fooles voyce is knowen by multitude of words.
text.NABRE: 5:3 When you make a vow to God, delay not its fulfillment. For God has no pleasure in fools; fulfill what you have vowed.

text.NRSV: When you make a vow to God, do not delay fulfilling it; for he has no pleasure in fools. Fulfill what you vow.
text.Rotherham: When thou vowest a vow unto God, do not defer to pay it, for there is no pleasure in dullards,—what thou vowest, pay!
text.KJV: 5:4 When thou vowest a vow vnto God, deferre not to pay it: for he hath no pleasure in fooles; pay that which thou hast vowed.
text.NABRE: 5:4 It is better not to make a vow than make it and not fulfill it.

text.NRSV: It is better that you should not vow than that you should vow and not fulfill it.
text.Rotherham: Better that thou shouldest not vow,—than vow, and not pay.
text.KJV: 5:5 Better is it that thou shouldest not vowe, then that thou shouldest vowe and not pay.
text.NABRE: 5:5 Let not your utterances make you guilty, and say not before his representative, “It was a mistake.” Why should God be angered by your words and destroy the works of your hands?

text.NRSV: Do not let your mouth lead you into sin, and do not say before the messenger that it was a mistake; why should God be angry at your words, and destroy the work of your hands?
text.Rotherham: Do not let thy mouth cause thy flesh to sin,—neither say thou, before the messenger, that it was, a mistake,—wherefore should God be indignant at thy voice, and destroy the work of thy hands?
text.KJV: 5:6 Suffer not thy mouth to cause thy flesh to sinne, neither say thou before the Angel, that it was an errour: wherefore should God be angrie at thy voyce, and destroy the worke of thine hands?
text.NABRE: 5:6 Despite many dreams, futilities, and a multitude of words, fear God!

text.NRSV: With many dreams come vanities and a multitude of words; but fear God.
text.Rotherham: For [it was done] amidst a multitude of dreams, and vanities, and many words,—but, towards God, be thou reverent.
text.KJV: 5:7 For in the multitude of dreames and many words, there are also diuers vanities: but feare thou God.
text.NABRE: 5:7 Gain and Loss of Goods. If you see oppression of the poor, and violation of rights and justice in the realm, do not be astonished by the fact, for the high official has another higher than he watching him and above these are others higher still—.

text.NRSV: If you see in a province the oppression of the poor and the violation of justice and right, do not be amazed at the matter; for the high official is watched by a higher, and there are yet higher ones over them.
text.Rotherham: If, the oppression of the poor, and the wresting of justice and righteousness, thou see in the province, do not be astonished over the matter,—for, one high above the highest, is watching, yea, the Most High, is over them.
text.KJV: 5:8 ¶ If thou seest the oppression of the poore, and violent peruerting of iudgement, and iustice in a prouince, maruell not at the matter: for he that is higher then the highest, regardeth, and there be higher then they.
text.NABRE: 5:8 But profitable for a land in such circumstances is a king concerned about cultivation.

text.NRSV: But all things considered, this is an advantage for a land: a king for a plowed field.
text.Rotherham: And, the profit of the earth, is, for all,—a king, by the field, is served.
text.KJV: 5:9 ¶ Moreouer the profit of the earth is for all: the king himselfe is serued by the field.
text.NABRE: 5:9 The covetous are never satisfied with money, nor lovers of wealth with their gain; so this too is vanity.

text.NRSV: The lover of money will not be satisfied with money; nor the lover of wealth, with gain. This also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: He that loveth silver, shall not be satisfied with silver nor, he that loveth abundance, with revenue,—even this, was vanity.
text.KJV: 5:10 Hee that loueth siluer shall not be satisfied with siluer; nor he that loueth abundance, with increase: this is also vanitie.
text.NABRE: 5:10 Where there are great riches, there are also many to devour them. Of what use are they to the owner except as a feast for the eyes alone?

text.NRSV: When goods increase, those who eat them increase; and what gain has their owner but to see them with his eyes?
text.Rotherham: When blessings are increased, increased are the eaters thereof,—what profit, then, to the owner of them saving the sight of his eyes?
text.KJV: 5:11 When goods increase, they are increased that eate them: and what good is there to the owners thereof, sauing the beholding of them with their eyes?
text.NABRE: 5:11 Sleep is sweet to the laborer, whether there is little or much to eat; but the abundance of the rich allows them no sleep.

text.NRSV: Sweet is the sleep of laborers, whether they eat little or much; but the abundance of the rich will not let them sleep.
text.Rotherham: Sweet the sleep of the labourer, whether, little or much, he eat,—but, the surfeit of the rich man, will not suffer him to sleep.
text.KJV: 5:12 The sleepe of a labouring man is sweete, whether he eate little or much: but the abundance of the rich will not suffer him to sleepe.
text.NABRE: 5:12 This is a grievous evil which I have seen under the sun: riches hoarded by their owners to their own hurt.

text.NRSV: There is a grievous ill that I have seen under the sun: riches were kept by their owner to his hurt,
text.Rotherham: Here was an incurable evil, I had seen under the sun, riches kept by the owner thereof, to his hurt;
text.KJV: 5:13 There is a sore euill which I haue seene vnder the Sun, namely riches kept for the owners therof to their hurt.
text.NABRE: 5:13 Should the riches be lost through some misfortune, they may have offspring when they have no means.

text.NRSV: and those riches were lost in a bad venture; though he is father of a son, he has nothing left.
text.Rotherham: and those riches perish, by being ill employed,—and though he begetteth a son, yet is there in his hand nothing at all.
text.KJV: 5:14 But those riches perish by euill trauell; and he begetteth a sonne, and there is nothing in his hand.
text.NABRE: 5:14 As they came forth from their mother’s womb, so again shall they return, naked as they came, having nothing from their toil to bring with them.

text.NRSV: As they came from their mother’s womb, so they shall go again, naked as they came; they shall take nothing for their toil, which they may carry away with them.
text.Rotherham: As he came from his mother’s womb, naked, he again departeth, as he came,—and, nothing, can he take of his toil, which he can carry in his hand.
text.KJV: 5:15 As he came forth of his mothers wombe, naked shall he returne to goe as he came, and shall take nothing of his labour, which he may carry away in his hand.
text.NABRE: 5:15 This too is a grievous evil, that they go just as they came. What then does it profit them to toil for the wind?

text.NRSV: This also is a grievous ill: just as they came, so shall they go; and what gain do they have from toiling for the wind,
text.Rotherham: Even this, moreover, is an incurable evil, altogether as he came, so, shall he depart,—what profit then shall he have who toileth for the wind?
text.KJV: 5:16 And this also is a sore euill, that in all points as he came, so shall hee goe: and what profit hath he that hath laboured for the winde?
text.NABRE: 5:16 All their days they eat in gloom with great vexation, sickness and resentment.

text.NRSV: besides spending all their days in darkness and grief, in much vexation and sickness and resentment?
text.Rotherham: Even all his days, [are spent] in darkness and mourning,—and he is very morose, and is sad and angry.
text.KJV: 5:17 All his dayes also hee eateth in darkenesse, and he hath much sorrowe, and wrath with his sicknesse.
text.NABRE: 5:17 Here is what I see as good: It is appropriate to eat and drink and prosper from all the toil one toils at under the sun during the limited days of life God gives us; for this is our lot.

text.NRSV: This is what I have seen to be good: it is fitting to eat and drink and find enjoyment in all the toil with which one toils under the sun the few days of the life God gives us; for this is our lot.
text.Rotherham: Lo! what, I myself, have seen—Better that it should be excellent to eat and to drink and to see blessedness, in all one’s toil wherein one toileth under the sun, for the number of the days of his life, in that God hath given it him, for, that, is his portion:
text.KJV: 5:18 ¶ Behold that which I haue seene: It is good and comely for one to eate and to drinke, and to enioy the good of all his labour that he taketh vnder the sunne, all the dayes of his life, which God giueth him: for it is his portion.
text.NABRE: 5:18 Those to whom God gives riches and property, and grants power to partake of them, so that they receive their lot and find joy in the fruits of their toil: This is a gift from God.

text.NRSV: Likewise all to whom God gives wealth and possessions and whom he enables to enjoy them, and to accept their lot and find enjoyment in their toil—this is the gift of God.
text.Rotherham: yet, as regardeth every man, to whom God hath given wealth and goods, and granted him power to eat thereof, and to take his portion, and to find gladness in his toil, this, is, the gift of God.
text.KJV: 5:19 Euery man also to whom God hath giuen riches and wealth, and hath giuen him power to eate thereof, and to take his portion, and to reioyce in his labour; this is the gift of God.
text.NABRE: 5:19 For they will hardly dwell on the shortness of life, because God lets them busy themselves with the joy of their heart.

text.NRSV: For they will scarcely brood over the days of their lives, because God keeps them occupied with the joy of their hearts.
text.Rotherham: Though it be not much, let him remember the days of his life,—for, God, beareth witness, by the gladness of his heart.
text.KJV: 5:20 For he shall not much remember the dayes of his life: because God answereth him in the ioy of his heart.

text.NRSV: There is an evil that I have seen under the sun, and it lies heavy upon humankind:
text.Rotherham: Here was an evil, I had seen under the sun,—and it is, common, among men:
text.KJV: 6:1 There is an euill which I haue seen vnder the Sun, and it is common among men:
text.NABRE: 6:1 Chapter 6 - Limited Worth of Enjoyment. There is another evil I have seen under the sun, and it weighs heavily upon humankind:

text.NRSV: those to whom God gives wealth, possessions, and honor, so that they lack nothing of all that they desire, yet God does not enable them to enjoy these things, but a stranger enjoys them. This is vanity; it is a grievous ill.
text.Rotherham: A man to whom God giveth riches and gains and honour, so that nothing doth he lack for his soul—of all that he craveth, and yet God doth not give him power to eat thereof, but, a man unknown, eateth it,—this, was vanity, and, an incurable evil, it was.
text.KJV: 6:2 A man to whom God hath giuen riches, wealth and honour, so that he wanteth nothing for his soule of all that he desireth, yet God giueth him not power to eate thereof, but a stranger eateth it: This is vanitie, and it is an euill disease.
text.NABRE: 6:2 There is one to whom God gives riches and property and honor, and who lacks nothing the heart could desire; yet God does not grant the power to partake of them, but a stranger devours them. This is vanity and a dire plague.

text.NRSV: A man may beget a hundred children, and live many years, so that they are many; but if he does not enjoy life’s good things, and has no burial, I say that a stillborn child is better off than he.
text.Rotherham: Though a man should beget a hundred children, and live, many years, so that many should be the days of his years but, his own soul, should not be satisfied with the good, and he should not even have, a burial, I said, Better than he, is an untimely birth!
text.KJV: 6:3 ¶ If a man beget an hundred children, and liue many yeeres, so that the dayes of his yeeres bee many: and his soule be not filled with good, and also that he haue no buriall, I say, that an vntimely birth is better then he.
text.NABRE: 6:3 Should one have a hundred children and live many years, no matter to what great age, still if one has not the full benefit of those goods, I proclaim that the child born dead, even if left unburied, is more fortunate.

text.NRSV: For it comes into vanity and goes into darkness, and in darkness its name is covered;
text.Rotherham: For, in vain, it came in, and, in darkness, it departeth,—and, with darkness, its name, is covered:
text.KJV: 6:4 For he commeth in with vanitie, and departeth in darkenesse, and his name shall be couered with darkenesse.
text.NABRE: 6:4 Though it came in vain and goes into darkness and its name is enveloped in darkness,

text.NRSV: moreover it has not seen the sun or known anything; yet it finds rest rather than he.
text.Rotherham: even the sun, it never saw, nor aught did it know,—more quietness, hath this than the other.
text.KJV: 6:5 Moreouer hee hath not seene the Sunne, nor knowen any thing: this hath more rest then the other.
text.NABRE: 6:5 though it has not seen the sun or known anything, yet the dead child has more peace.

text.NRSV: Even though he should live a thousand years twice over, yet enjoy no good—do not all go to one place?
text.Rotherham: Even though one hath lived a thousand years twice told, yet, good, hath he not seen,—is it not, unto one place, that, all, are going?
text.KJV: 6:6 ¶ Yea though he liue a thousand yeeres twice told, yet hath he seene no good: Doe not all goe to one place?
text.NABRE: 6:6 Should such a one live twice a thousand years and not enjoy those goods, do not both go to the same place?

text.NRSV: All human toil is for the mouth, yet the appetite is not satisfied.
text.Rotherham: All the toil of man, is for his mouth,—though, even the desire, is not satisfied!
text.KJV: 6:7 All the labour of man is for his mouth, and yet the appetite is not filled.
text.NABRE: 6:7 All human toil is for the mouth, yet the appetite is never satisfied.

text.NRSV: For what advantage have the wise over fools? And what do the poor have who know how to conduct themselves before the living?
text.Rotherham: For what profit hath the wise man, over the dullard? What can, the poor man, know—so as to walk before the living?
text.KJV: 6:8 For what hath the wise more then the foole? what hath the poore, that knoweth to walke before the liuing?
text.NABRE: 6:8 What profit have the wise compared to fools, or what profit have the lowly in knowing how to conduct themselves in life?

text.NRSV: Better is the sight of the eyes than the wandering of the desire; this also is vanity and a chasing after wind.
text.Rotherham: Better what the eyes behold, than the wandering of desire,—even this, was vanity, and a feeding on wind.
text.KJV: 6:9 ¶ Better is the sight of the eyes, then the wandering of the desire: this is also vanitie and vexation of spirit.
text.NABRE: 6:9 “What the eyes see is better than what the desires wander after.” This also is vanity and a chase after wind.

text.NRSV: Whatever has come to be has already been named, and it is known what human beings are, and that they cannot contend with one who is stronger than they.
text.Rotherham: Whatsoever one may be, long ago, was he called by his name, and it is known that it is—Son of Earth,—he cannot, therefore, contend with one stronger than he.
text.KJV: 6:10 That which hath bene, is named already, and it is knowen that it is man: neither may he contend with him that is mightier then he.
text.NABRE: 6:10 II. Qoheleth’s Conclusions - Whatever is, was long ago given its name, and human nature is known; mortals cannot contend in judgment with One who is stronger.

text.NRSV: The more words, the more vanity, and how is one the better?
text.Rotherham: Seeing there are things in abundance which make vanity abound, what profit hath man?
text.KJV: 6:11 ¶ Seeing there be many things that increase vanitie, what is man the better?
text.NABRE: 6:11 For the more words, the more vanity; what profit is there for anyone?

text.NRSV: For who knows what is good for mortals while they live the few days of their vain life, which they pass like a shadow? For who can tell them what will be after them under the sun?
text.Rotherham: For who knoweth what is good for a man throughout his life, for the number of the days of his life of vanity, seeing he will make them, like a shadow,—for who can tell a man, what shall be after him, under the sun?
text.KJV: 6:12 For who knoweth what is good for man in this life, all the dayes of his vaine life, which he spendeth as a shadow? for who can tell a man what shal be after him vnder the sunne?
text.NABRE: 6:12 For who knows what is good for mortals in life, the limited days of their vain life, spent like a shadow? Because who can tell them what will come afterward under the sun?

text.NRSV: A good name is better than precious ointment, and the day of death, than the day of birth.
text.Rotherham: Better a name, than precious ointment,—and the day of death, than the day of one’s birth.
text.KJV: 7:1 A Good name is better then precious ointment: and the day of death, then the day of ones birth.
text.NABRE: 7:1 A. No One Can Find Out the Best Way of Acting Chapter 7 Critique of Sages on the Day of Adversity - A good name is better than good ointment, and the day of death than the day of birth.

text.NRSV: It is better to go to the house of mourning than to go to the house of feasting; for this is the end of everyone, and the living will lay it to heart.
text.Rotherham: Better to go to the house of mourning, than to go to the house of banqueting, for, that, is the end of all men,—and, the living, should take it to his heart.
text.KJV: 7:2 ¶ It is better to goe to the house of mourning, then to goe to the house of feasting: for that is the end of all men, and the liuing will lay it to his heart.
text.NABRE: 7:2 It is better to go to the house of mourning than to the house of feasting, For that is the end of every mortal, and the living should take it to heart.

text.NRSV: Sorrow is better than laughter, for by sadness of countenance the heart is made glad.
text.Rotherham: Better is grief than laughter,—for, by the marring of the face, amended is the heart.
text.KJV: 7:3 Sorrow is better then laughter: for by the sadnesse of the countenance the heart is made better.
text.NABRE: 7:3 Sorrow is better than laughter; when the face is sad, the heart grows wise.

text.NRSV: The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning; but the heart of fools is in the house of mirth.
text.Rotherham: The heart of the wise, is in the house of mourning, but, the heart of dullards, in the house of mirth.
text.KJV: 7:4 The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning: but the heart of fooles is in the house of mirth.
text.NABRE: 7:4 The heart of the wise is in the house of mourning, but the heart of fools is in the house of merriment.

text.NRSV: It is better to hear the rebuke of the wise than to hear the song of fools.
text.Rotherham: Better to hear the rebuke of the wise, than for any man to hear the song of dullards,
text.KJV: 7:5 It is better to heare the rebuke of the wise, then for a man to heare the song of fooles.
text.NABRE: 7:5 It is better to listen to the rebuke of the wise than to listen to the song of fools;

text.NRSV: For like the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the laughter of fools; this also is vanity.
text.Rotherham: For, as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so, is the laughter of the dullard,—even this, then, was vanity.
text.KJV: 7:6 For as the crackling of thornes vnder a pot, so is the laughter of the foole: this also is vanitie.
text.NABRE: 7:6 For as the crackling of thorns under a pot, so is the fool’s laughter. This also is vanity.

text.NRSV: Surely oppression makes the wise foolish, and a bribe corrupts the heart.
text.Rotherham: For, oppression, maddeneth the wise,—and a bribe, destroyeth the understanding.
text.KJV: 7:7 ¶ Surely oppression maketh a wise man mad: and a gift destroyeth the heart.
text.NABRE: 7:7 Extortion can make a fool out of the wise, and a bribe corrupts the heart.

text.NRSV: Better is the end of a thing than its beginning; the patient in spirit are better than the proud in spirit.
text.Rotherham: Better the latter end of a thing, than the beginning thereof,—Better a patient spirit, than a haughty spirit.
text.KJV: 7:8 Better is the ende of a thing then the beginning thereof: and the patient in spirit is better then the proude in spirit.
text.NABRE: 7:8 Better is the end of a thing than its beginning; better is a patient spirit than a lofty one.

text.NRSV: Do not be quick to anger, for anger lodges in the bosom of fools.
text.Rotherham: Do not be rash in thy spirit, to be indignant,—for, indignation, in the bosom of dullards, doth remain.
text.KJV: 7:9 Be not hastie in thy spirit to bee angry: for anger resteth in the bosome of fooles.
text.NABRE: 7:9 Do not let anger upset your spirit, for anger lodges in the bosom of a fool.

text.NRSV: Do not say, “Why were the former days better than these?” For it is not from wisdom that you ask this.
text.Rotherham: Do not say, What hath happened, that, the former days, were better than these? for, not wisely, askest thou concerning this.
text.KJV: 7:10 Say not thou, What is the cause that the former dayes were better then these? For thou doest not enquire wisely concerning this.
text.NABRE: 7:10 Do not say: How is it that former times were better than these? For it is not out of wisdom that you ask about this.

text.NRSV: Wisdom is as good as an inheritance, an advantage to those who see the sun.
text.Rotherham: Good is wisdom, with an inheritance,—and a profit, to such as see the sun.
text.KJV: 7:11 ¶ Wisedome is good with an inheritance: and by it there is profite to them that see the sunne.
text.NABRE: 7:11 Wisdom is as good as an inheritance and profitable to those who see the sun.

text.NRSV: For the protection of wisdom is like the protection of money, and the advantage of knowledge is that wisdom gives life to its possessor.
text.Rotherham: For, a protection, is wisdom, and, a protection, is silver,—but, the advantage of knowledge, is, that, wisdom, giveth life to the possessors thereof.
text.KJV: 7:12 For wisedome is a defence, and money is a defence: but the excellencie of knowledge is, that wisedome giueth life to them that haue it.
text.NABRE: 7:12 For the protection of wisdom is as the protection of money; and knowledge is profitable because wisdom gives life to those who possess it.

text.NRSV: Consider the work of God; who can make straight what he has made crooked?
text.Rotherham: Consider the work of God,—for who can straighten what he hath bent?
text.KJV: 7:13 Consider the worke of God: for who can make that straight, which hee hath made crooked?
text.NABRE: 7:13 Consider the work of God. Who can make straight what God has made crooked?

text.NRSV: In the day of prosperity be joyful, and in the day of adversity consider; God has made the one as well as the other, so that mortals may not find out anything that will come after them.
text.Rotherham: In the day of prosperity, be joyful, but, in the day of misfortune, consider,—Even the one equally with the other, hath God made, to the end man might find out—after him—nothing.
text.KJV: 7:14 In the day of prosperitie be ioyfull, but in the day of aduersitie consider: God also hath set the one ouer against the other, to the end that man should find nothing after him.
text.NABRE: 7:14 On a good day enjoy good things, and on an evil day consider: Both the one and the other God has made, so that no one may find the least fault with him.

text.NRSV: In my vain life I have seen everything; there are righteous people who perish in their righteousness, and there are wicked people who prolong their life in their evildoing.
text.Rotherham: Everything, had I seen, in my days of vanity,—Here was a righteous man, perishing in his righteousness, and there was a lawless man, continuing long in his wickedness.
text.KJV: 7:15 All things haue I seene in the dayes of my vanitie: there is a iust man that perisheth in his righteousnes, and there is a wicked man that prolongeth his life in his wickednes.
text.NABRE: 7:15 Critique of Sages on Justice and Wickedness. I have seen all manner of things in my vain days: the just perishing in their justice, and the wicked living long in their wickedness.

text.NRSV: Do not be overrighteous, and do not make yourself overwise; why should you destroy yourself?
text.Rotherham: Do not become so very righteous, neither count thyself wise beyond measure,—wherefore shouldst thou destroy thyself?
text.KJV: 7:16 Be not righteous ouer much, neither make thy selfe ouer wise: why shouldest thou destroy thy selfe?
text.NABRE: 7:16 “Be not just to excess, and be not overwise. Why work your own ruin?

text.NRSV: Do not be overwicked, and do not be a fool; why should you die before your time?
text.Rotherham: Do not be so very lawless, neither become thou foolish,—wherefore shouldst thou die, before thy time?
text.KJV: 7:17 Be not ouermuch wicked, neither be thou foolish: why shouldest thou die before thy time?
text.NABRE: 7:17 Be not wicked to excess, and be not foolish. Why should you die before your time?”

text.NRSV: It is good that you should take hold of the one, without letting go of the other; for the one who fears God shall succeed with both.
text.Rotherham: It is well that thou shouldst lay fast hold of this, but, even from the other, do not withdraw thy hand,—for, he that revereth God, shall come forth out of them all.
text.KJV: 7:18 It is good that thou shouldest take holde of this, yea also from this withdraw not thine hand: for hee that feareth God, shall come foorth of them all.
text.NABRE: 7:18 It is good to hold to this rule, and not to let that one go; but the one who fears God will succeed with both.

text.NRSV: Wisdom gives strength to the wise more than ten rulers that are in a city.
text.Rotherham: Wisdom, bringeth more strength to a wise man, than ten heroes, that are in the city.
text.KJV: 7:19 Wisedome strengtheneth the wise, more then ten mightie men which are in the citie.
text.NABRE: 7:19 Wisdom is a better defense for the wise than ten princes in the city,

text.NRSV: Surely there is no one on earth so righteous as to do good without ever sinning.
text.Rotherham: For, as for men, there is none righteous in the earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not.
text.KJV: 7:20 For there is not a iust man vpon earth, that doeth good, and sinneth not.
text.NABRE: 7:20 yet there is no one on earth so just as to do good and never sin.

text.NRSV: Do not give heed to everything that people say, or you may hear your servant cursing you;
text.Rotherham: Moreover, not to all the words which men speak, do thou apply thy heart,—lest thou hear thine own servant reviling thee!
text.KJV: 7:21 Also take no heede vnto all words that are spoken; lest thou heare thy seruant curse thee.
text.NABRE: 7:21 Do not give your heart to every word that is spoken; you may hear your servant cursing you,

text.NRSV: your heart knows that many times you have yourself cursed others.
text.Rotherham: For truly, many times, thy heart knoweth,—that, even thou thyself, hast reviled others.
text.KJV: 7:22 For often times also thine owne heart knoweth, that thou thy selfe likewise hast cursed others.
text.NABRE: 7:22 for your heart knows that you have many times cursed others.

text.NRSV: All this I have tested by wisdom; I said, “I will be wise,” but it was far from me.
text.Rotherham: All this, have I proved by wisdom,—I said, I will be wise, but, that, was far from me.
text.KJV: 7:23 ¶ All this haue I prooued by wisedome: I said, I will be wise, but it was farre from me.
text.NABRE: 7:23 All these things I probed in wisdom. I said, “I will acquire wisdom”; but it was far beyond me.

text.NRSV: That which is, is far off, and deep, very deep; who can find it out?
text.Rotherham: Far away, is that which hath been,—and deep, deep, who can find it out?
text.KJV: 7:24 That which is farre off, and exceeding deepe, who can finde it out?
text.NABRE: 7:24 What exists is far-reaching; it is deep, very deep: Who can find it out?

text.NRSV: I turned my mind to know and to search out and to seek wisdom and the sum of things, and to know that wickedness is folly and that foolishness is madness.
text.Rotherham: Resolved, I, in my heart, to know and search out, and to seek wisdom, and a conclusion,—and to know lawlessness [to be] stupidity, and folly to be madness.
text.KJV: 7:25 I applyed mine heart to know, and to search, and to seeke out wisdome, and the reason of things, aud to know the wickednes of folly, euen of foolishnesse and madnesse.
text.NABRE: 7:25 I turned my heart toward knowledge; I sought and pursued wisdom and its design, and I recognized that wickedness is foolishness and folly is madness.

text.NRSV: I found more bitter than death the woman who is a trap, whose heart is snares and nets, and whose hands are fetters; one who pleases God escapes her, but the sinner is taken by her.
text.Rotherham: I, could indeed find, to be, more bitter than death, the woman, whose heart is, snares and nets, and her hands, bonds,—whoso is pleasing before God, shall escape from her, but, he that sinneth, shall be captured by her.
text.KJV: 7:26 And I finde more bitter then death, the woman whose heart is snares & nets, and her handes as bands: who so pleaseth God, shall escape from her, but the sinner shall be taken by her.
text.NABRE: 7:26 Critique of Advice on Women. More bitter than death I find the woman who is a hunter’s trap, whose heart is a snare, whose hands are prison bonds. The one who pleases God will be delivered from her, but the one who displeases will be entrapped by her.

text.NRSV: See, this is what I found, says the Teacher, adding one thing to another to find the sum,
text.Rotherham: See! this, have I found, saith the Proclaimer [counting] one by one, to find a conclusion;
text.KJV: 7:27 Behold, this haue I found (saith the Preacher) counting one by one to finde out the account:
text.NABRE: 7:27 See, this have I found, says Qoheleth, adding one to one to find the sum.

text.NRSV: which my mind has sought repeatedly, but I have not found. One man among a thousand I found, but a woman among all these I have not found.
text.Rotherham: what my soul still sought, yet I found not,—one man out of a thousand, have I found, but, a woman among all these, have I not found.
text.KJV: 7:28 Which yet my soule seeketh, but I finde not: one man among a thousand haue I found, but a woman among all those haue I not found.
text.NABRE: 7:28 What my soul still seeks and has yet to find is this: “One man out of a thousand have I found, but a woman among them all I have not found.”

text.NRSV: See, this alone I found, that God made human beings straightforward, but they have devised many schemes.
text.Rotherham: Only, see, this, have I found, That God made man upright, but, they, have sought out many devices.
text.KJV: 7:29 Loe, this onely haue I found, that God hath made man vpright: but they haue sought out many inuentions.
text.NABRE: 7:29 But this alone I have found: God made humankind honest, but they have pursued many designs.

text.NRSV: Who is like the wise? And who knows the interpretation of a thing? Wisdom illumines the face, and the hardness of one’s countenance is changed.
text.Rotherham: Who is really a wise man, and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing? The wisdom of a man, lighteth up his countenance, but, by defiance of countenance, one is disfigured.
text.KJV: 8:1 Who is as the Wise man? and who knoweth the interpretation of a thing? a mans wisedome maketh his face to shine, and the boldnes of his face shalbe changed.
text.NABRE: 8:1 Chapter 8 Critique of Advice to Heed Authority - Who is like the wise person, and who knows the explanation of things? Wisdom illumines the face and transforms a grim countenance.

text.NRSV: Keep the king’s command because of your sacred oath.
text.Rotherham: I [said], The bidding of the king, observe thou, even out of regard to the oath of God.
text.KJV: 8:2 I counsell thee, to keepe the kings commandement, and that in regard of the oath of God.
text.NABRE: 8:2 Observe the command of the king, in view of your oath to God.

text.NRSV: Do not be terrified; go from his presence, do not delay when the matter is unpleasant, for he does whatever he pleases.
text.Rotherham: Not rashly from his presence, shouldst thou go: do not take thy stand in a vexatious thing,—for, whatsoever he pleaseth, he will do.
text.KJV: 8:3 Bee not hastie to goe out of his sight: stand not in an euill thing, for he doeth whatsoeuer pleaseth him.
text.NABRE: 8:3 Be not hasty to withdraw from the king; do not persist in an unpleasant situation, for he does whatever he pleases.

text.NRSV: For the word of the king is powerful, and who can say to him, “What are you doing?”
